top_candidates.reasoning: The engineering layer explicitly invokes the wind-boat metaphor ('the wind has only to enter the sail') with the boat-with-intent framing — this is verbatim the verse's structure (vāyur nāvam ivāmbhasi). The 'one un-restrained input stream is enough' line directly mirrors the verse's grammatical singular yan (one sense, not all senses), which is a specific contribution of 2.67 not present in 2.58, 2.60, 2.61, or 2.68. The Sanskrit term prajñā is named explicitly in the body. The eroded-prajñā-as-stolen-judgment frame anchors on harati prajñām. The analog could not be from any other verse without losing these three specific anchors.

Adjacent verse on senses carrying off the mind, similar theme. 2.60 says 'pramāthīni indriyāṇi haranti prasabhaṁ manaḥ' — the senses violently carry off the mind. The analog's 'eroded prajñā' / 'stolen judgment' framing rhymes with haranti, but 2.60's specific contribution is the violently (prasabham) and 'even of the wise person striving' (yatato hy api kaunteyaḥ puruṣasya vipaścitaḥ) — neither of which is in this analog. Distant possibility.

BG 2.66 (nāsti buddhir ayuktasya — the unyoked has no buddhi) shares the un-yoked-mind theme. But 2.66 has no boat metaphor and no input-stream specificity; it is a more abstract claim about the absence of buddhi without yukti. Distant possibility ranked third for completeness.
